findings.summary: _OpenAIClientHolder in common/llm.py is a module-level mutable singleton shared across threads via import-time side effect
evidence: _openai_holder = _OpenAIClientHolder() at module scope (line 43) is mutated by init_openai_client() called from library_setup.setup_libraries(); all providers access it implicitly through _create_completion via the mixin
findings.suggestion: Pass the OpenAI client explicitly through Settings or a context object rather than relying on module-level mutable state; alternatively, make the holder truly thread-safe with a lock around init/get

findings.summary: llm_limiter is another module-level mutable singleton requiring coordinated init before use
evidence: llm_limiter = LLMConcurrencyLimiter() at module scope (line 40); must be initialized via llm_limiter.init() in bootstrap_daemon before any provider calls; no guard against double-init or use-before-init beyond a None check
findings.suggestion: Consider making the limiter part of a daemon context/runtime object passed to providers, eliminating the implicit initialization ordering requirement

findings.summary: classifier/__init__.py re-exports 5 symbols creating a facade that obscures internal module structure
evidence: __init__.py imports ClassificationProcessor, ClassificationProvider, ClassificationResult, TaxonomyCache, parse_classification_response; however, no caller imports from the package root -- daemon.py and worker.py import directly from submodules
findings.suggestion: Remove the re-exports from __init__.py since no code uses them, or add a comment documenting the intended public API if they serve an external integration purpose

findings.summary: The openai SDK is used as the HTTP client for Ollama, coupling the project to OpenAI's SDK even when OpenAI is not the provider
evidence: library_setup.py creates an openai.OpenAI client with api_key='dummy' and base_url pointing to Ollama (lines 28-40); this works because Ollama exposes an OpenAI-compatible API, but ties the project to the openai SDK's behavior and version constraints even for non-OpenAI deployments
findings.suggestion: This is a reasonable pragmatic choice given Ollama's compatibility, but document the dependency rationale; consider abstracting the LLM client interface if a non-OpenAI-compatible provider is ever needed

findings.summary: OCR and classifier daemon entry points (daemon.py) follow identical structure but without shared abstraction
evidence: Both daemon.py files follow the same pattern: bootstrap_daemon() -> log startup params -> create client/provider -> run_polling_threadpool() -> close clients. The classifier version adds taxonomy_cache but otherwise the structure is copy-pasted with minor variations
findings.suggestion: The duplication is modest (two files) and the polling loop is already shared in daemon_loop.py; if a third daemon is added, extract a common daemon runner that accepts a factory for the worker

findings.summary: Tags cross module boundaries as inconsistent types: set[int], list[int], and Iterable[int]
evidence: extract_tags() returns set[int]; clean_pipeline_tags() returns set[int]; but update_document_metadata() accepts tags via **kwargs and internally calls list(value) (paperless.py line 189)
classifier/worker.py line 205 passes list(updated) to update_document_metadata; ocr/worker.py line 202 passes set directly to update_document which expects Iterable[int]
stale_lock.py line 45 wraps tags in list() before passing to update_document_metadata
findings.suggestion: Standardize on set[int] internally and convert to list[int] only at the PaperlessClient boundary; this would remove scattered list() conversions and make the tag contract explicit

findings.summary: The retry decorator implicitly requires the decorated method's object to have a .settings attribute satisfying RetrySettings
evidence: retry() decorator accesses self.settings (line 33) on the first positional argument, creating an invisible contract. The Protocol RetrySettings is defined but never used in a type annotation on the decorator signature -- it is only documentary
findings.suggestion: Make settings explicit: either accept it as a decorator argument or annotate the self parameter with the Protocol to make the contract visible to type checkers

findings.summary: Settings.__init__ manually parses 30+ environment variables with repetitive int(os.getenv(...)) patterns
evidence: Lines 106-107: int(os.getenv('PRE_TAG_ID', '443')), repeated for POST_TAG_ID, POLL_INTERVAL, MAX_RETRIES, etc.
Lines 136-139: max(1, int(os.getenv(...))) pattern repeated for OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS, DOCUMENT_WORKERS
Lines 155-165: max(0, int(os.getenv(...))) pattern repeated for all CLASSIFY_* settings
findings.suggestion: Extract a _get_int_env(name, default, min_value=None) helper to replace the repeated int(os.getenv(...)) + max() pattern, reducing the 30+ lines of boilerplate

findings.summary: OcrProcessor.process() is 70+ lines with deeply nested try/finally/try/except blocks
evidence: process() method spans lines 50-135 with nested try/finally for claim, nested try/finally for image cleanup, and a conditional flow with multiple early returns -- the method mixes orchestration, error routing, timing, and logging concerns
findings.suggestion: Extract the image-acquisition-and-OCR phase into a separate method (e.g., _acquire_and_ocr_pages) to reduce nesting depth in the main process() method

findings.summary: Settings class uses class-level type annotations that look like dataclass fields but are actually uninitialized; real values are set imperatively in __init__
evidence: Lines 17-61 declare type annotations (PAPERLESS_URL: str, etc.) on the class body that are never used as default values -- they serve only as documentation. The actual attributes are set in _load_*() methods. This creates a misleading appearance of a dataclass or attrs class
findings.suggestion: Either convert to a proper dataclass/NamedTuple with a factory classmethod, or remove the class-body annotations and rely on the __init__ assignments alone to avoid the visual mismatch

findings.summary: ClassificationProcessor.process() orchestrates 9 distinct steps mixing I/O, validation, taxonomy resolution, and metadata updates
evidence: process() method (lines 66-152) performs: refresh document, check error tag, claim lock, check content, check refusal markers, truncate content, call LLM, validate result, apply classification -- each step has distinct failure modes handled inline
findings.suggestion: The method is well-commented and the steps are sequential, but extracting the validation phase (lines 106-141) into a _validate_and_classify() method would improve readability and testability

findings.summary: OpenAIChatMixin couples stat tracking, retry logic, and LLM client access into a single mixin class
evidence: OpenAIChatMixin bundles _init_stats(), reset_stats(), get_stats(), and _create_completion() -- providers must inherit it and also define _STAT_KEYS and have self.settings. The mixin accesses the global _openai_holder directly (line 84)
findings.suggestion: Consider splitting stats tracking from the LLM call logic; the stats concern is orthogonal to the chat completion concern and could be a separate ThreadSafeStats instance owned by each provider directly

findings.summary: init_openai_client() and get_openai_client() are thin forwarding functions around _openai_holder
evidence: init_openai_client (line 46-47) forwards to _openai_holder.init(); get_openai_client (line 50-51) forwards to _openai_holder.get(); is_openai_client_ready (line 54-55) forwards to _openai_holder.is_ready() -- three 1-line functions that add no policy
findings.suggestion: These functions serve as a stable public API for the module, which is a valid facade pattern; however, they could be replaced by exposing the holder instance directly since the method names are already self-documenting

findings.summary: e2e test files define local _make_settings helpers instead of using the shared make_settings_obj from tests/helpers/factories.py
evidence: test_ocr_workflow.py lines 15-32 defines _make_settings() that wraps make_settings_obj with OCR-specific defaults
test_paperless.py lines 17-25 defines _make_settings() that wraps make_settings_obj with test-specific defaults
This pattern is repeated in multiple test files, each with slightly different default overrides, making it hard to understand the baseline test configuration
findings.suggestion: Create domain-specific preset factories in tests/helpers/factories.py (e.g., make_ocr_settings, make_classifier_settings) to centralize the defaults and reduce duplication

findings.summary: test_ocr_workflow.py redefines _make_mock_ocr_provider locally instead of using the shared make_mock_ocr_provider from tests/helpers/mocks.py
evidence: tests/e2e/test_ocr_workflow.py lines 69-86 define _make_mock_ocr_provider with nearly identical behavior to tests/helpers/mocks.make_mock_ocr_provider; the e2e version adds transcribe_side_effect support but this could be added to the shared version
findings.suggestion: Extend the shared make_mock_ocr_provider in tests/helpers/mocks.py to accept transcribe_side_effect and use it in the e2e tests

findings.summary: PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS is a broad 5-type tuple used inconsistently: some callers catch it and log, others let exceptions propagate
evidence: PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS = (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError, KeyError) -- catches almost everything
tags.py functions catch PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS and log+continue (best-effort)
claims.py catches PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS and returns False
worker.py process() does NOT catch exceptions from paperless_client.get_document() (line 64) or download_content() (line 82) -- these propagate to the thread pool
This creates an inconsistent contract: callers cannot predict which Paperless operations are fault-tolerant and which are fatal
findings.suggestion: Document which operations are best-effort (tag cleanup, lock release) vs. fatal (initial document fetch, download) and consider narrowing PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS or using separate exception tuples for each strategy

findings.summary: ValueError is raised for both configuration errors and JSON parse failures, making it hard for callers to distinguish
evidence: config.py raises ValueError for invalid LLM_PROVIDER (line 81), empty AI_MODELS (line 207), bad MAX_RETRIES (line 126), etc.
result.py raises ValueError for empty classification response (line 55) and non-dict response (line 59)
Both are caught by PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS which includes ValueError, so a config validation error could be silently swallowed if it occurs at runtime
findings.suggestion: Introduce a ConfigurationError subclass for config validation failures to distinguish them from runtime parse errors

findings.summary: Image.close() failures are silently swallowed with only a warning log
evidence: Lines 100-104: except OSError catches image close failures and logs a warning but continues; while acceptable for cleanup, the narrow OSError catch means other exceptions from close() would propagate unexpectedly
findings.suggestion: Widen the catch to Exception for cleanup operations, or use a contextlib.suppress(Exception) pattern

findings.summary: run_polling_threadpool has no dedicated test for the outer error-recovery loop or shutdown signal handling
evidence: daemon_loop.py lines 99-121 contain the main polling loop with _DAEMON_LOOP_EXCEPTIONS catch-and-continue logic and shutdown signal checking; this is the core reliability mechanism of the daemon but relies on integration/e2e scenarios to exercise it
findings.suggestion: Add unit tests that verify: (1) the loop continues after a caught exception, (2) the loop exits on is_shutdown_requested(), (3) the sleep function is called between iterations

findings.summary: Worker tests rely heavily on patching module-level imports, creating tight coupling between tests and implementation details
evidence: Nearly every test method patches 3-5 module-level functions: @patch('ocr.worker.release_processing_tag'), @patch('ocr.worker.claim_processing_tag'), @patch('ocr.worker.bytes_to_images'), @patch('ocr.worker.assemble_full_text'), @patch('ocr.worker.get_latest_tags')
Renaming or moving any of these imports would break multiple tests without any production behavior change
findings.suggestion: Consider injecting collaborators (claim function, image converter) through the constructor or a protocol, reducing the need for import-path patching

findings.summary: bootstrap_daemon orchestrates 6 setup steps but test coverage appears to only verify individual components
evidence: bootstrap_daemon() calls Settings(), configure_logging(), setup_libraries(), register_signal_handlers(), llm_limiter.init(), and run_preflight_checks() in sequence; failure in any step should return None. The function is critical path for daemon startup
findings.suggestion: Add integration-level tests that verify the full bootstrap sequence handles various failure modes (bad config, preflight failure, etc.)

findings.summary: Several simple functions have docstrings that restate the function name without adding insight
evidence: shutdown.py: request_shutdown, is_shutdown_requested, reset_shutdown are 1-line functions with no docstrings but self-documenting names -- this is fine
tags.py line 164: clean_pipeline_tags docstring 'Return a copy of tags with all automation-pipeline tag IDs removed' restates what tags - pipeline_tag_ids(settings) already communicates
llm.py line 89-91: unique_models docstring 'Deduplicate a model list while preserving insertion order' restates what dict.fromkeys already communicates to Python developers
findings.suggestion: Remove docstrings that restate the implementation for 1-3 line functions; keep docstrings that explain WHY or document non-obvious behavior

findings.summary: Tag management functions log at multiple verbosity levels for routine operations
evidence: claim_processing_tag logs at 4 different points: exception on refresh (line 32), info on already-claimed (line 41), exception on claim failure (line 53), exception on verify failure (line 65), info on success (line 83) -- 5 log statements for a single tag-add operation
release_processing_tag logs exception on refresh failure and exception on release failure -- 2 log statements for removing a tag
findings.suggestion: The logging density is warranted for distributed lock semantics where debugging is critical; however, consider reducing the already-claimed log from info to debug level since it is a normal expected condition
